danger_signals.visual_form: White-armored enemy sprites in imperial uniform
danger_signals.meaning: Active Imperial threat; enemy engagement expected

danger_signals.visual_form: Large space station environment; industrial grey corridors
First-person perspective with floating debris obstacles
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazard requiring shields; transition state between safe and hostile zones
Failure state; direct connection to Imperial authority/ultimate antagonist

safety_signals.visual_form: R2-D2, C-3PO, Obi-Wan Kenobi, Han Solo, Princess Leia roster display
safety_signals.meaning: Progression toward objective completion; party assembly indicates safety advancement

safety_signals.visual_form: Desert landscape with sandcrawler and cave structures
safety_signals.meaning: Safe staging area; quest hub for objective collection
Preparation for dangerous Millennium Falcon section; safety buffer mechanism

safety_signals.signal: Multiple character lives (Luke) vs. limited lives (Han, Leia)
safety_signals.meaning: Luke = primary safe character; Han/Leia = high-risk variants

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Tatooine (starting safe zone); Death Star detention block (rescue objective); Rebel fighter escort endgame

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Stormtroopers (common soldiers); Darth Vader (Game Over boss archetype)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Death Star interior; asteroid field defense perimeter; grey industrial corridors

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Ground-level desert enemies distinct from Imperial forces

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Desert hub environment; scattered objective markers (R2-D2, Obi-Wan cave, Mos Eisley bar, Han Solo); light enemy density
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Luke operates with multiple lives; allies available for recruitment; player agency in order selection

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: First-person cockpit perspective; floating debris obstacles; shield meter display
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: High-risk traversal; shield depletion mechanic creates resource pressure; Millennium Falcon vulnerability

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Industrial grey corridors; increased stormtrooper density; Princess Leia detention block; tractor beam generator structure
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Final objective sequence; high enemy engagement; rescue mechanics activated; culmination combat path

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Rebel fighter escort scenes; Death Star destruction sequence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player transitions from infiltration to support role; external narrative closure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character portrait greyed out or removed from HUD
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Obi-Wan resurrection mechanic activated; player must manage limited resurrection uses; increased difficulty signal

environmental_storytelling.example: Tatooine locations (Sandcrawler, cave system, Mos Eisley bar) serve as visual narrative waypoints that communicate plot progression without explicit dialogue
Shield collection in Tatooine levels prepares player for upcoming Asteroid field; visual cue of preparedness
R2-D2, Obi-Wan, Han Solo, Leia collection creates visible party growth indicating narrative advancement toward Rebel base/Death Star assault
Tatooine (organic, desert) → Millennium Falcon (tech interior) → Death Star (industrial grey) = visual journey from refuge to enemy heartland
Tusken Raiders on Tatooine vs. Stormtroopers on Death Star communicates faction territorial control through enemy spawn patterns
C-3PO and R2-D2 HUD presence indicates information/navigation support available; tool-like visual roles

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Multiple lives; rapid movement; lightsaber combat capability; primary protagonist agency
Single life constraint; optional replacement for Luke; indicates high-risk variant playstyle
Single life; dual role as objective and playable character; vulnerability indicator for endgame stakes
Resurrection mechanic; passive utility; five-use limited resource indicating late-game value scarcity
Death Star map provision; navigation aid; passive information role
Predictable patrol patterns; ranged attack behavior; high spawn density in Imperial zones
Territorial threat; desert-specific spawn; distinct combat pattern from Imperial forces
Game Over screen exclusive; visual-only appearance; authority embodiment rather than combatable boss

notes: Star Wars (NES 1991) communicates world state primarily through environmental architecture (Tatooine safe hub → Death Star hostile fortress) and enemy type distribution (native vs. Imperial forces). Character recruitment mechanics create visible narrative progression. The game lacks explicit faction color-coding but distinguishes Imperial presence through stormtrooper white armor and industrial Death Star aesthetic versus organic Tatooine environments. Danger is signaled through enemy density scaling and resource depletion mechanics (shields, lives, resurrections). World state transitions are marked by gameplay perspective shifts (side-scroll to first-person in asteroid field) and environmental visual style changes. Obi-Wan's limited resurrection uses (5) visually communicate late-game resource scarcity. No dynamic world-state UI (no faction reputation system, no political meter) is documented—state changes are largely narrative/environmental rather than mechanically displayed.
